罗宾汉（Robinhood）周一因20岁的客户亚历克斯·凯恩斯（Alex Kearns）的家人被起诉，罪名是不当死亡。阿里克斯·凯恩斯（Alex Kearns）去年夏天去世，因为他相信自己已经在千禧一代青睐的股票交易应用上蒙受了巨大损失。
 ＆＃34;此案例的重点在于罗宾汉（Robinhood）的进取策略和策略，以诱使包括Alex在内的经验不足且不老练的投资者以诱人的利润来承担巨大的风险，＆＃34;他的父母丹·多萝西·基恩斯和他的妹妹悉尼·基恩斯在圣克拉拉的加利福尼亚州法院提起了申诉。该家庭位于伊利诺伊州内珀维尔。
  Robinhood的鲁re行为直接或直接导致了其中一名受害者的死亡，投诉说。该诉讼还指控经纪人过失造成了情绪困扰和不公平的商业行为。
 亚历克斯·凯恩斯（Alex Kearns）当时是内布拉斯加大学林肯分校的二年级学生，他认为自己在Robinhood上的现金余额为负730,165美元，因此他于6月自杀。
 投诉称，凯恩斯误解了罗宾汉的财务报表，并保护他的家人免于承担财务义务。
 该诉讼称，Kearns曾就大型水下平衡问题三度尝试联系Robinhood客户服务。
   在CNBC看到给家人的一封信中，凯恩斯（Kearns）指控罗宾汉（Robinhood）允许他承担过多的风险。他声称自己购买的看跌期权和出售的股票应该已经注销，＆＃34;根据说明。 
认沽权是赋予所有者权利以指定价格出售证券的期权。
 交易员说他不知道。根据说明，他在做什么。
 ＆＃34;一个20岁没有收入的人怎么能获得价值近一百万美元的杠杆作用？阅读Kearns给他的家人写的便条。 ＆＃34;无意分配这么多钱，冒这么大的风险，我只是以为我要冒我实际拥有的钱的风险。
 罗宾汉（Robinhood）发言人告诉CNBC，亚历克斯·凯恩斯（Alex Kearns）毁了我们。死亡。自6月以来，我们已经对期权产品进行了改进。
  Robinhood已成为首次投资者进入股票市场的热门切入点。它已从2016年的100万用户增长到去年春季的1300万以上。在Reddit投资者推动的GameStop戏剧中，流量分析网站SameWeb估计，仅在一月份，就有300万用户下载了Robinhood。
 由首席执行官弗拉德·特内夫（Vlad Tenev）运营的Robinhood因其“游戏化”而受到审查。投资和涉嫌掠夺性营销实践。
 在该应用决定在最近的GameStop争议期间限制某些证券的交易之后，Robinhood还面临着来自客户的集体诉讼。该经纪公司计划于2021年上市，该公司一再表示，其大部分用户是长期投资者。 
Robinhood是2020年零售贸易热潮的最大受益者之一，它也受到了对其客户的访问的严格审查，而无需接受适当的投资教育。去年，马萨诸塞州监管机构针对Robinhood提起诉讼，指责该交易应用对缺乏经验的投资者实施掠夺性营销。
 证券交易委员会（Securities and Exchange Commission）于12月指控该经纪公司误导客户有关股票交易应用程序如何赚钱以及未能兑现承诺的最佳交易执行的误导。
 基恩斯大学家庭投诉说，罗宾汉不仅允许亚历克斯开户，而且当年亚历克斯在大学一年级时，还允许他交易期权。
 更糟的是，Robinhood几乎没有提供任何投资指导，并且它的客户＆＃39;服务＆＃39;几乎不存在，由没有任何人际接触或互动的自动电子邮件回复组成，＆＃34;一家人涉嫌诉讼。
  ＆＃34;亚历克斯·基恩斯（Alex Kearns）毁了我们死亡。自6月以来，我们对选件进行了改进。其中包括增加在应用程序中执行合同的能力，指导以帮助客户及早完成任务，更新我们如何显示购买力，提供更多关于期权的教育材料以及新的财务标准和针对寻求交易第3级新客户的经修订的经验要求选项。在12月初，我们还为具有未结期权头寸或最近到期的客户增加了实时语音支持，并计划扩展到其他用例。我们还更改了协议，以提升通过电子邮件向我们发送有关锻炼和早期分配帮助的客户的信息。我们始终致力于使Robinhood成为负责任地学习和投资的地方。＆＃34;

note_en:
Robinhood was sued Monday for wrongful death by the family of Alex Kearns, a 20-year-old customer who took his life last summer after believing he had racked up big losses on the millennial-favored stock trading app.
 "This case centers on Robinhood's aggressive tactics and strategy to lure inexperienced and unsophisticated investors, including Alex, to take big risks with the lure of tantalizing profits," said the complaint filed by his parents Dan and Dorothy Kearns, and his sister Sydney Kearns in a California state court in Santa Clara. The family is based in Naperville, Illinois.
  Robinhood's "reckless conduct directly and proximately caused the death of one of its victims," the complaint said. The lawsuit is also accusing the brokerage of negligent infliction of emotional distress and unfair business practices.
 Alex Kearns, a then-sophomore at the University of Nebraska at Lincoln, committed suicide in June after thinking he had a negative $730,165 cash balance on Robinhood.
 The complaint alleges that Kearns misunderstood the Robinhood financial statement and was protecting his family from the financial obligation.
 The suit says that Kearns made three attempts to contact Robinhood customer service regarding the massive underwater balance.
   In a note to his family that CNBC has seen, Kearns accused Robinhood of allowing him to pile on too much risk. He claimed the puts he bought and the shares sold "should have cancelled out," according to the note.
 Puts are options that give the owner the right to sell a security at a specified price.
 The trader said he had "no clue" what he was doing, according to the note.
 "How was a 20 year old with no income able to get assigned almost a million dollars worth of leverage?" read the note Kearns wrote to his family. "There was no intention to be assigned this much and take this much risk, and I only thought that I was risking the money that I actually owned."
 A Robinhood spokesperson told CNBC, "We were devastated by Alex Kearns' death. Since June, we've made improvements to our options offering."
  Robinhood has become a popular entry point to the stock market for first-time investors. It has grown from 1 million users in 2016 to more than 13 million last spring. Amid the Reddit investor-fueled GameStop drama, traffic analysis site SimilarWeb estimates 3 million more users downloaded Robinhood in January alone.
 Robinhood, which is run by CEO Vlad Tenev, has come under scrutiny for its "gamification" of investing and alleged predatory marketing practices.
 Robinhood is also facing class-action lawsuits from clients after the app's decision to restrict trading in certain securities during the recent GameStop controversy. The brokerage firm, which has plans to go public in 2021, has repeatedly said that the majority of its users are long-term investors.
 Robinhood, one of the biggest beneficiaries of the retail trading boom in 2020, has also come under scrutiny for the access it gives its clients without proper investing education. Last year,  Massachusetts regulators filed a complaint against Robinhood, accusing the trading app of predatory marketing on inexperienced investors.
 The Securities and Exchange Commission  charged the brokerage in December with misleading customers about how the stock-trading app makes money and failing to deliver the promised best execution of trades.
 The Kearns' family complaint says, "Not only did Robinhood permit Alex to open the account, but when Alex was a freshman in college later that year, it permitted him to trade options."
 "Worse, Robinhood provided almost no investment guidance, and its customer 'service' was virtually non-existent, consisting of automated e-mail replies devoid of any human contact or interaction," the family alleged in the suit.
  "We were devastated by Alex Kearns' death. Since June, we've made improvements to our options offering. These include adding the ability to exercise contracts in the app, guidance to help customers through early assignment, updates to how we display buying power, more educational materials on options, and new financial criteria and revised experience requirements for new customers seeking to trade Level 3 options. In early December, we also added live voice support for customers with an open options position or recent expiration, and plan to expand to other use cases. We also changed our protocol to escalate customers who email us for help with exercise and early assignment. We remain committed to making Robinhood a place to learn and invest responsibly."

title: 罗宾汉（Robinhood）被自杀的20岁商人家庭起诉
title_en: Robinhood Sued By Family of 20-Year-Old Trader Who Committed Suicide

怪癖和夸克·分析大学的研究人员已经将普通的木板转变成透明的材料，这种材料几乎和玻璃一样透明，但是强度更高并且具有更好的绝缘性能。将来可能成为节能的建筑材料。
  木材由两种基本成分制成：纤维素（纤细的纤维）和木质素（将这些纤维粘合在一起以增强强度）。
   将纸巾撕成两半，并沿边缘仔细看。您会看到少量纤维素纤维粘着。木质素是一种类似胶的材料，将纤维粘合在一起，有点像玻璃纤维或碳纤维中的塑料树脂。木质素还包含称为生色团的分子，该分子使木材呈棕色并阻止光通过。
  早期制造透明木材的尝试涉及去除木质素，但这涉及危险化学品，高温和大量时间，使产品昂贵且有些脆。新技术既便宜又容易，可以在后院完成。
  从一米长，一毫米厚的木板开始，科学家简单地使用普通的油漆刷在过氧化氢溶液上刷过。当在阳光下或在紫外线灯下放置约一个小时时，过氧化物会漂白掉棕色的发色团，但木质素保持完整，因此木材变成白色。
    接下来，他们在木材中注入了一种坚硬的透明环氧树脂，该环氧树脂专为航海使用而设计，该环氧树脂填充了木材中的空隙和孔隙，然后硬化。这使白木透明。
  您可以通过将同一条纸巾浸入水中并将其放在有图案的表面上来看到类似的效果。穿过水和纤维素纤维的光会使白色毛巾纸变得半透明，而不会被折射散射。 
木材中的环氧树脂做得更好，可以让90％的可见光通过。结果是一块长得像玻璃的东西，具有木材的强度和柔韧性。
    作为窗户材料，它将更能防止意外损坏。透明木材比玻璃轻，具有更好的隔热性能，这很重要，因为窗户是建筑物中热损失的主要来源。由于不涉及高温，因此制造透明木材所需的能源也可能更少。
  在节能建筑中，透明木材可能会成为玻璃的替代品，或者在恶劣环境下可能成为太阳能电池板的覆盖物。使用可能没有尽头。
      从轻木到橡木的许多不同类型的木材都可以制成透明的，无论是沿着木纹还是靠木纹切割都没关系。如果将透明木材做得更厚一点，它将足够坚固以成为建筑物结构的一部分，因此可能会有整个透明木墙。
  尽管这项技术尚未扩大到工业水平，但研究人员表示，它作为一种新型建筑材料具有巨大潜力。实际上，他们说从理论上讲，整个房子可以做成透明的。尚不清楚为什么有人会想住在透明的房子里，但是对于那些愿意的人来说，扔石头是可以的……
note_en:
Quirks & Quarks  · Analysis      Researchers at the University of Maryland have turned ordinary sheets of wood into   transparent material that is nearly as clear as glass, but stronger and with better insulating properties. It could become an energy efficient building material in the future.
  Wood is made of two basic ingredients: cellulose, which are tiny fibres, and lignin, which bonds those fibres together to give it strength.
   Tear a paper towel in half and look closely along the edge. You will see the little cellulose fibres sticking up. Lignin is a glue-like material that bonds the fibres together, a little like the plastic resin in fibreglass or carbon fibre. The lignin also contains molecules called chromophores, which give the wood its brown colour and prevent light from passing through.
  Early attempts to make transparent wood involved removing the lignin, but this involved hazardous chemicals, high temperatures and a lot of time, making the product expensive and somewhat brittle. The new technique is so cheap and easy it could literally be done in a backyard.
  Starting with planks of wood a metre long and one millimetre thick, the scientists simply brushed on a solution of hydrogen peroxide using an ordinary paint brush. When left in the sun, or under a UV lamp for an hour or so, the peroxide bleached out the brown chromophores but left the lignin intact, so the wood turned white.
    Next, they infused the wood with a tough transparent epoxy designed for marine use, which filled in the spaces and pores in the wood and then hardened. This made the white wood transparent.
  You can see a similar effect by taking that same piece of paper towel, dip half of it in water and place it on a patterned surface. The white paper towel will become translucent with light passing through the water and cellulose fibres without being scattered by refraction.
  The epoxy in the wood does an even better job, allowing 90 per cent of visible light to pass through. The result is a long piece of what looks like glass, with the strength and flexibility of wood.
    As window material, it would be much more resistant to accidental breakage. The clear wood is lighter than glass, with better insulating properties, which is important because windows are a major source of heat loss in buildings. It also might take less energy to manufacture clear wood because there are no high temperatures involved.
  Transparent wood could become an alternative to glass in energy efficient buildings, or perhaps coverings for solar panels in harsh environments. There could be no end of uses.
      Many different types of wood, from balsa to oak, can be made transparent, and it doesn't matter if it is cut along the grain or against it. If the transparent wood is made a little thicker, it would be strong enough to become part of the structure of a building, so there could be entire transparent wooden walls.
  While this technology has yet to  be scaled up to industrial levels, the researchers say it has great potential as a new building material. In fact, they say that theoretically, an entire house could be made transparent. It is not clear why anyone would want to live in a transparent house, but for people who do, it would be OK to throw stones…
title: 科学家开发出比玻璃更坚固，更轻的透明木材
title_en: Scientists Develop Transparent Wood That Is Stronger, Lighter Than Glass

巴黎—在法国因未能达成其签署并以其首都命名的历史性协议所承诺的气候变化目标而被判有罪之后，四个环保组织正在哀求胜利。巴黎行政法庭周三裁定，法国未履行其根据2015年《巴黎协定》作出的承诺减少温室气体的承诺，对生态破坏负有责任。
   法院宣布政府无所作为，但驳回了提起诉讼的四个非政府组织提出的损害赔偿要求，命令政府只向他们支付象征性的欧元。法庭还表示，将在两个月内决定是否建议政府采取任何措施解决其未能履行其承诺的问题。
   前绿党领导人和内阁大臣塞西尔·杜夫洛特（CécileDuflot）现在是乐施会法国的负责人，该组织是将政府拉上法庭的四个非政府组织之一，本周称这是具有象征意义的裁决，气候正义的历史性胜利。＆＃34;
  法国乐施会，法国绿色和平组织和两个法国环保组织一起对政府提起诉讼。两年前，他们组织了一份请愿书，谴责他们所谓的“气候无所作为”。由法国国家。在短短一个月内，他们就获得了200万个签名，并在2019年3月提起诉讼，声称未能采取行动。
 在2015年12月签署《巴黎气候协定》时，法国承诺到2030年将温室气体排放量与1990年的水平相比减少40％，并在2050年之前实现碳中和。去年，法国决定推迟这一承诺。
  周三的裁决对法国总统埃曼纽尔·马克龙（Emmanuel Macron）造成了打击，他将应对气候变化作为其政府的当务之急。对于监督谈判和签署《巴黎协定》的国家而言，这也是一个明显的耳光。共有196个国家和地区在法国首都达成协议，采取措施将全球变暖的幅度限制在远低于工业化前水平的2摄氏度，最好是1.5摄氏度以下。
  法院的判决也是政治上的胜利，法国绿色和平组织负责人让·弗朗索瓦·朱利亚德（Jean-FrançoisJulliard）表示，马克龙再也不能袖手旁观了。 
法国政府发表声明说，它已经注意到了。 法院的裁决，承认尚未实现最初的目标，并承诺下个月将在议会辩论一项新的解决气候问题的法案。 政府说，这项立法将构成加快法国生态过渡的新的决定性步骤。  冠状病毒 大流行 已经看到 许多国家 蹒跚而 在他们的 巴黎 协定 的承诺， 但 已有一些 新的乐观态度 ，因为 新当选的 美国 总统拜登 签署了一项行政命令 把 跟随他的 前任 ＆＃ 39 美国 回 协议 ; S 退出。
title: 法国因未能履行巴黎气候协定承诺而被判有罪
title_en: France Found Guilty of Failing To Meet Its Paris Climate Accord Commitments

辉瑞公司希望随着产量的增加和效率的提高，将COVID-19疫苗的生产时间缩短近50％
    辉瑞公司告诉《今日美国》，辉瑞公司希望将生产一批COVID-19疫苗所需的时间从110天缩短到平均60天，减少了一半左右，因为它可以使生产过程更高效，更有效地进行生产。
 随着国家提高疫苗接种计划，增加疫苗接种可能有助于缓解疫苗短缺造成的瓶颈。
 ＆＃34;我们将其称为“项目光速”，这是有原因的，辉瑞公司无菌注射剂业务副总裁Chaz Calitri说，他在密西根州卡拉马祖市经营该公司的工厂。 ＆＃34;就在上个月，我们的产量翻了一番。
 疫苗生产咨询公司免疫政策和知识翻译部门总裁罗伯特·范·埃森（Robert Van Exan）表示，提高速度和容量并不意外。
 “没有人生产过如此大规模的mRNA疫苗，因此，您可以打赌制造商正在学习的最低价格。我敢打赌，他们每天都会遇到一些疫苗挑战，每天他们都会解决它，这也成为他们的工作手册，”他说。
   辉瑞的COVID-19疫苗是在辉瑞的三家工厂生产的：从密苏里州切斯特菲尔德开始，迁至马萨诸塞州安多弗，最后在密歇根州卡拉马祖完成。截至周六，全国已注射约2,060万剂辉瑞疫苗。 
辉瑞公司说，辉瑞公司的生产系统是基于实验室中疫苗的开发方式而定的。通常，工程师会花费数年时间来提高效率和成本效益。那不是COVID-19发生的事情。
  疫苗小瓶开始下线后，工程师便开始分析生产如何更快更好地工作。
   生产速度越来越快。例如，首先制作开始疫苗过程的DNA需要16天；很快将花费9或10。尽管质量控制和测试已经加快，但公司官员表示，仍在满足FDA法规和最佳生产规范。
 除了提高速度外，辉瑞还通过在所有三家工厂增加生产线来增加产量。
  ＆＃34;我们开展业务的方式将发生深刻的变化，＆＃34;卡利特里谈到了自从他的老板于2020年3月20日第一次给他打电话以来的经历，并说卡拉马祖工厂将在疫苗的快速生产中发挥关键作用。
 我们只是向自己证明了我们可以从三月份的电话发展到现在提供50至6000万剂。
note_en:
Pfizer expects to cut COVID-19 vaccine production time by close to 50% as production ramps up, efficiencies increase
    Pfizer expects to nearly cut in half the amount of time it takes to produce a batch of COVID-19 vaccine from 110 days to an average of 60 as it makes the process more efficient and production is built out, the company told USA TODAY.
 As the nation revs up its vaccination programs, the increase could help relieve bottlenecks caused by vaccine shortages.
 "We call this 'Project Light Speed,' and it's called that for a reason," said Chaz Calitri, Pfizer's vice president for operations for sterile injectables, who runs the company's plant in Kalamazoo, Michigan. "Just in the last month we've doubled output."
 The increased speed and capacity is not unexpected, said Robert Van Exan, president of Immunization Policy and Knowledge Translation, a vaccine production consulting firm.
 “Nobody’s ever produced mRNA vaccines at this scale, so you can bet your bottom dollar the manufacturers are learning as they go. I bet you every day they run into some vaccine challenge and every day they solve it, and that goes into their playbook,” he said.
   Pfizer's COVID-19 vaccine is made at threePfizer plants: starting in Chesterfield, Missouri, moving to Andover, Massachusetts, and finishing in Kalamazoo, Michigan. As of Saturday, about 20.6 million doses of the  Pfizer vaccine had been administered nationwide.
 Pfizer based its production system on how the vaccine was developed in the laboratory, Calitri said. Normally engineers would spend years improving efficiencies and cost-effectiveness. That's not what happened with COVID-19.
  As soon as vials of vaccine began coming off the production line, engineers started analyzing how production could work faster and better.
   Production is getting faster. For example, making the DNA that starts the vaccine process first took 16 days; soon it will take nine or 10. Though quality control and testing has accelerated, company officials say FDA regulations and best manufacturing practices are still being met.
 Along with improving speed, Pfizer also is increasing output by adding manufacturing lines in all three plants.
  "There are going to be profound shifts in the way we do business," Calitri said of what he's experienced since  his boss first called him on March 20, 2020 and said the Kalamazoo plant would play a key role in the rapid production of the vaccine.
 "We just demonstrated to ourselves that we can go from a phone call in March to having now delivered 50 to 60 million doses."
title: 辉瑞正在将Covid-19疫苗的产量提高一倍
title_en: Pfizer Is Doubling Its Output of Covid-19 Vaccines

Facebook首席执行官马克·扎克伯格（Mark Zuckerberg）致力于人们可以传送的科学未来，而不是从一个地方到另一个地方进行通勤。他出现在仅受邀的Clubhouse应用程序上，详细介绍了Facebook在虚拟现实（VR）和增强现实（AR）技术方面的进展。
  以＆＃39; Zuck23＆＃39;扎克伯格说：“我们应该传送，而不是传送，”并暗示Facebook的Reality Labs正在进行这项工作。
     ＆＃34; [VR]可以解锁的一件事就是可以生活在您想要的任何地方，并可以出现在另一个地方，并且真的感觉就像您在这里一样，＆＃34; Facebook首席执行官表示。它将释放大量的经济机会，因为人们将能够生活在他们想要的地方，并能够在他们想要的地方越来越多地工作，并且可以将其传送到适当的地方。对于减少人们上班途中的通勤量，我也很乐观。我认为，电动汽车在减少排放方面的进步是令人兴奋的，但我告诉我的团队，更容易移动一些原子，因此我们应该传送而不是开车。
  Clubhouse是一个新的社交媒体平台，仅用于音频播放，用户只能在受邀时参与，它是世界上最热门的新社交网络。实际上，扎克伯格参加了《好时光秀》。该应用程序中的脱口秀节目令许多人感到惊讶，他在那儿对VR和AR技术进行了约15分钟的广泛讨论，深入了解了Facebook的Reality Labs正在进行的研究。
     扎克伯格建议将VR和AR技术结合起来实现梦想。虚拟现实可以利用整个显示器并使用户沉浸在不同的世界中，而AR则允许用户将事物放置在他们周围的物理世界中，从而在周围呈现真实的画面。
  他举例说明了增强现实将如何帮助解决导致Google Glass失败的问题，他说，必须使该技术在社会上得到人们的接受，并将其小型化为厚框眼镜。
     扎克伯格说，他的实验室在基础技术方面的进展一直稳定，这些基础技术将借助VR和AR彻底改变旅行的未来。 ＆＃34;使所有图形和视觉系统真正感觉像您沉浸在空间中。要使VR变得像您在VR上所希望的那样现实，需要走很长的技术道路。他说。 
鉴于封锁，远程工作人员重申无需物理办公室，并表示Facebook计划在本世纪末借助VR技术允许50％的Facebook人员远程工作。
note_en:
Facebook CEO Mark Zuckerberg has pitched for scientific future where people can teleport, instead of commuting physically from one place to the other. Appearing on the invite-only Clubhouse app, he detailed Facebook's progress in virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR) technology.
  Under the name 'Zuck23,' Zuckerberg said, "We should be teleporting, not transporting," and hinted that work is going on at Facebook's Reality Labs.
     "One of the things that [VR] will unlock is the ability to live anywhere you want and be present in another place and really feel like you are there," the Facebook CEO said."It is going to unlock a lot of economic opportunity because people will be able to live where they want and increasingly work where they want and kind of teleport into place. I am also pretty optimistic about the impact on climate, in reducing the amount of commuting that people have to do. I think the advance in electric cars in reducing emissions is great and exciting, but I tell my team that it is easier to move bits of atoms around so we should be teleporting, not driving."
  Clubhouse is a new social media platform that is audio-only and users can participate only on invitation and it claims to be "the world's buzziest new social network." In fact, Zuckerberg's participation in 'The Good Time Show,' a talk show in the app came as a surprise for many, where he discussed extensively for about 15 minutes on VR and AR technology, giving insights into the research being undertaken at Facebook's Reality Labs.
     Zuckerberg suggested a combination of both VR and AR technologies to achieve the dream of teleportation. While VR that makes use of the entire display and immerses users in a different world, AR allows users to place things into the physical world around them, giving a reality picture around.
  Giving the example of how AR would help solve the problem that led to the failure of Google Glass, he said the technology will have to be made socially acceptable and miniaturized into thick-rimmed glasses.
     Zuckerberg said the progress is steady at his labs on foundational technologies that will revolutionize the future of travel wit the help of both VR and AR. "There is getting all the graphics and visual systems to really feel like you are immersed in the space. There's a long path of technology that needs to get done to kind of get that to be as realistic as you would want on VR," he said.
  In view of the  lockdowns, the remote workin has reiterated that there is no need for physical offices and said Facebook is planning to allow 50 percent of the  Facebook staff to work remotely by the end of this decade with the help of VR technology.
title: 马克·扎克伯格希望通勤取代VR / AR
title_en: Mark Zuckerberg Wants Commuting Replaced with VR/AR

note_en:
(CNN)You've probably heard of 3D printed  face masks and even  3D printed hands, as 3D printing technology has expanded over the past few decades.
 Now a company  says it has listed the first 3D printed house in the United States for sale. The Riverhead, New York, home is listed online through  Zillow with an asking price of $299,999.
 "This is the future, there is no doubt about it," says Kirk Andersen, the director of operations at  SQ4D Inc.
      "What we want to do is print homes fast, and cheap and strong," Andersen said.
  The company can set up its Autonomous Robotic Construction System at a build site in six to eight hours. It then lays concrete layer by layer, creating footing, the foundation of a house and the interior and exterior walls of the structure.
  3D printed houses could also end up having a radical effect on the wallets of potential buyers in America.
 "The cost of construction is 50% cheaper than the cost of comparable newly-constructed homes in Riverhead, New York, and 10 times faster," said Stephen King, the Zillow Premier agent who has the 3D house listing.
 The 3D printed house will include 1,407 square feet of living space and will be built with concrete. The home has three bedrooms, two baths and a 2.5-car detached garage.
  "I want people to not be afraid of automation...it is just a different tool and different method. But it's still the same product; we are still building a house at the end of the day," Andersen says.
 An open house, held for the 3D home on Saturday, featured the company's already built  demo home.
  Andersen and King say the Riverhead home is the first 3D printed home to receive a certificate of occupancy in the United States. That wasn't easy because of strict local building codes, they said.
 "We did it in one of the hardest places and there's a beauty in that because that means we can eventually do it anywhere," Andersen said.
   "We can make things more affordable and safer. We can use the technology to tackle homelessness, and aid in disaster relief in an eco-friendly way," Andersen said.
 Correction : An earlier version of this story misspelled the last name of Kirk Andersen, the director of operations at SQ4D Inc.
title: 首批3D打印房屋发售，预示着更快，更便宜的房屋建造
title_en: First 3D-Printed House Goes On Sale, Foreshadowing Faster, Cheaper Homebuilding

周三晚些时候，受欢迎的Reddit留言板WallStreetBets的主持人在聊天应用程序Discord上发布了几个屏幕截图。他们表明，其他主持人已经悄悄地开始谈论达成电影交易。
 “我们削减了什么？”根据屏幕截图，其中一位主持人在Discord聊天中询问。
 到星期四早上，对好莱坞财富的追求已变成一场丑陋的战斗，让人们瞥见了一个突然成名的Reddit社区的不守规矩的性质。
 当时正考虑电影交易的WallStreetBets主持人开始引导其他主持人质疑这些主持人是否暗中试图从论坛的成功中获利。最终，Reddit的员工加倍努力以平息动乱。
 “你们都可以跟我讨论发生了什么吗？”根据与《纽约时报》分享的对话截图，一名网名为“ Sypoppop”的Reddit员工问道。
 WallStreetBets的战斗是过去10天内困扰华尔街的一系列在线投资者的传奇故事中的最新转折。在留言板上的帖子推动下，参与者敦促彼此购买视频游戏零售商GameStop的股票，该公司的股票表现异常出色。市场狂热吸引了对赌GameStop股票的对冲基金，这是民粹主义的举动，很快就吸引了主流的想象。
 自那以来，GameStop的股价一直在剧烈波动。周四，该股暴跌了42％。 
自从2012年以来，WallStreetBets就一直处于混乱状态，该市场自2012年以来一直在Reddit上使用，成千上万的人用来交易股票提示和聊天特定投资。上个月开始出现GameStop狂潮后，还有数百万加入了留言板。某一时刻，论坛的设置被设为“私人”（这意味着帖子无法公开查看），因为它被如此多的关注所淹没。
 视频零售商GameStop的股价已从1月份的高位暴跌，这是由社交媒体上的模因推动的。
 一月份在Reddit上互相交易的业余交易员大量押注了该公司的股票，使价格一度上涨了1,700％以上。
 这一浪潮的部分目的是伤害那些一直做空的大型对冲基金-押注于GameStop股票。其中一些基金因此遭受了巨大的损失。
 但是，许多增加股票投资的个人投资者也可能损失巨额资金。一些人认为，即使股价暴跌，价格也将回升并拒绝出售。
 现在，监管机构正在研究反弹的开始方式以及是否应因此而制定新规则。
 到星期四下午，WallStreetBets已拥有850万会员，其中绝大多数是在GameStop之后加入的。 
“这绝对是巨大的，”现年35岁的洛杉矶居民尼克·科米尔（Nick Cormier）在过去四年的大部分时间里一直是WallStreetBets的常客，他谈到了新用户和关注的涌入。 “即使在过去的一周里，这个数字也是天文数字。”
 主持在线社区日常管理的主持人是所有Reddit董事会的核心。在自愿的基础上，他们筛选了成千上万的评论，禁止违反规则和起草准则的用户。一些主持人还建立了自定义技术，例如自动漫游器，以帮助小组更平稳地运行。 WallStreetBets主持人的数量随着时间的推移而波动，但最近几周出现了几十名。
 WallStreetBets由39岁的Jaime Rogozinski于2012年成立，他是墨西哥城的信息技术顾问。
 他上个月对TMZ表示：“我正在寻找一个社区，让人们以一种毫无歉意的方式谈论高风险交易的场所，使人们以可支配收入赚一些短期钱，”
 但是到去年年初，罗戈津斯基先生一直在努力保持控制。他被卷入与一个贸易团体的关系之争中，该贸易团体赞助了一个留言板活动，导致对内幕交易和偏颇的股票推介的指控。去年四月，Rogozinski先生及其社区内的盟友被罢免。
  在上周，几位对留言板具有管理控制权的高级主持人在Discord上的私人聊天室开会，讨论了因突然成名而产生的商机。
 一位主持人说，他与这本书的作者Ben Mezrich保持联系，该书后来成为电影“社交网络”，根据上周的截图，他上周获得了写书并协助制作有关GameStop传奇的电影的交易。论坛与时报共享。 
“我想我们该快点走，”另一位主持人回信说。 “尽管工作室在竞争。”
 接受《泰晤士报》采访的六位主持人中，没有一个愿意提供真实姓名，但《泰晤士报》证实，这些人控制着董事会的主持人帐户。
 Rogozinski先生宣布本周他已将自己故事的版权出售给电影制片厂，此后对话开始升温。 Rogozinski先生未回应置评请求。
 该小组的一位长期主持人zzz看到了对话并提出了问题。他在所有主持人的更广泛的聊天室中张贴了对话的图像。
 zjz在给《泰晤士报》的电子邮件中写道：“我们突然发现这些以前不活跃的主持人正试图*从字面意义上*出售他们如何建立子目录并破坏我们的故事。”
 在周三晚上发布给WallStreetBets的帖子中，该帖子很快被删除，zjz还写道：“我们被顶级mod劫为人质。他们离开了很多年，闻到钱回来了。”
 这导致了越来越多的谴责和侮辱，很快就超出了电影协议。一些人开始批评高层主持人为提高知名度而采取的措施，例如创建Twitter帐户和雇用公共关系代表。一些人还提出了死亡威胁。 
周三末和周四初，高级版主开始撤出提出问题的低级版主。
 当到达其新的Twitter帐户时，最高主持人表示他们想达成电影协议，但正计划将任何收益捐给慈善机构。
 主持人写道：“他们试图使我们看起来像是在抓现金，这是不诚实的。”
 高层主持人说，这场战斗还植根于他们对董事会现代化的渴望，考虑到所有已经开始使用董事会的新人。他们说，像zjz这样的反对主持人想要保持WallStreetBets过去的那种朴实的感觉。
 主持人说：“这是他一生的工作，他担心这种情况会发生根本变化。”
  zjz在电子邮件中写道：“这是我们与所有朋友聚会的地方，我们放任自流，并坦诚地为赚钱而存在，而不是为了互相出售任何东西。”
 在星期四的下午，Reddit介入，删除了WallStreetBets的主要主持人。他们使站在zjz身边的主持人重新控制，尽管zjz本人并未得到恢复。 
自3月份因在一家专门从事游戏《魔术聚会》的商店失职而失业以来，科米尔（Cormier）表示，他对WallStreetBets的战斗感到沮丧。  他担心论坛会崩溃，几乎不可能发起上周引起如此惊人市场反应的那种协调努力。  他说：“如果他们杀死了WallStreetBets，那么重新开始这项工作将非常困难。”
note_en:
Late on Wednesday, a moderator of the popular Reddit message board WallStreetBets posted several screenshots on the chat app Discord. They showed that other moderators had quietly started talking among themselves about landing a movie deal.
 “What’s our cut?” one of the moderators had asked in a Discord chat, according to the screenshots.
 By Thursday morning, that quest for Hollywood riches had exploded into an ugly battle, giving a glimpse into the unruly nature of a suddenly famous Reddit community.
 That was when the WallStreetBets moderators who were considering the film deal began booting out other moderators who had questioned them for secretly trying to profit from the forum’s success. Eventually, employees at Reddit weighed in to try to quell the unrest.
 “Can you all discuss with me what is going on?” a Reddit employee with the screen name sodypop asked, according to screenshots of the conversation shared with The New York Times.
 The WallStreetBets fight is the latest twist in the saga of an online army of investors who have  roiled Wall Street over the past 10 days. Fueled by posts on the message board, where participants urged one another to buy the stock of the video-game retailer  GameStop, the company’s shares went on an  extraordinary run. The market frenzy entrapped hedge funds that had bet against GameStop’s stock, a populist move that soon captured mainstream imagination.
 GameStop’s shares have since gyrated wildly. On Thursday, the stock plunged 42 percent.
 The chaos has put a spotlight on WallStreetBets, which has been on Reddit since 2012 and which millions use to trade stock tips and chat about specific investments. Once the GameStop mania began last month, millions more joined the message board. At one point, the forum’s settings were set to private — meaning that posts could not be publicly viewed — because it was so overwhelmed by the flood of attention.
 Shares in GameStop, the video retailer,  have crashed from their January highs, which were driven by memes on social media.
 Amateur traders egging on one another on Reddit  bet heavily on shares of the company in January, sending the price up more than 1,700 percent at one point.
 The wave was in part aimed at  hurting large hedge funds that had been  short selling — betting against — GameStop stock. Some of those funds  experienced huge losses as a result.
 But many of the individual investors who pumped up the stock could lose huge amounts of money, too. Some  believe the price will go back up and are refusing to sell, even as the share price has collapsed.
 Now, regulators are looking into  how the rally started and whether  new rules should be created because of it.
 By Thursday afternoon, WallStreetBets had over 8.5 million members, the vast majority of whom had joined in the wake of GameStop.
 “It’s definitely huge,” Nick Cormier, 35, a Los Angeles resident who has been a regular lurker on WallStreetBets for most of the last four years, said of the influx of new users and attention. “The amount even this past week has been astronomical.”
 Moderators, who handle the day-to-day management of the online community, are central to all Reddit boards. On a volunteer basis, they sift through thousands of comments, bar users who break the rules and draft guidelines. Some moderators also build custom technology, such as automated bots, to help the group run more smoothly. The number of WallStreetBets moderators has fluctuated over time, but in recent weeks there have been a few dozen.
 WallStreetBets was  founded in 2012 by Jaime Rogozinski, 39, an information technology consultant in Mexico City.
 “I was looking for a community, a place for people to talk about high-risk trades in an unapologetic way for people to make some short-term money with disposable income,” he told  TMZ last month.
 But by early last year, Mr. Rogozinski was struggling to remain in control. He became embroiled in a controversy over ties to a trading group that had sponsored an event for the message board, leading to  accusations of insider trading and biased stock promotion. Last April, Mr. Rogozinski and his allies in the community were ousted.
  Over the last week, several top moderators, who have administrative control of the message board, met in a private chat room on Discord to discuss the business opportunities arising from their sudden fame.
 One moderator said he was in touch with Ben Mezrich, an author of the book that became the movie “The Social Network,” who last week secured deals to write a book and help with a movie about the GameStop saga, according to screenshots from the forum shared with The Times.
 “Oof we gotta go fast i think,” another moderator wrote back. “While the studios are competing.”
 None of the six moderators The Times interviewed were willing to give their real names, but The Times verified the people were in control of the board’s moderator accounts.
 The conversation heated up after Mr. Rogozinski announced that he had  sold the rights to his own story to a movie studio this week. Mr. Rogozinski did not respond to requests for comment.
 One longtime moderator of the group, known as zjz, saw the conversation and took issue. He posted images of the conversation in a broader chat room for all the moderators.
 “We suddenly find out these formerly inactive moderators are trying to *literally* sell the story of how they built the subreddit and undermine us,” zjz wrote in an email to The Times.
 In a post to WallStreetBets on Wednesday night, which was quickly removed, zjz also wrote: “We’ve been taken hostage by the top mods. They left for years and came back when they smelled money.”
 That led to escalating recriminations and insults that soon went beyond a movie deal. Some began criticizing the top moderators for moves they had made to raise their profile, like creating a Twitter account and hiring a public relations representative. Some also made death threats.
 Late Wednesday and early Thursday, the top moderators began removing lower-ranking moderators who were asking questions.
 When reached on their new  Twitter account, the top moderators said they wanted to strike a movie deal, but were planning to give any proceeds to charity.
 “Them trying to make it look like we are cash grabbing is so dishonest,” the moderators wrote.
 The top moderators said that the battle was also rooted in their desire to modernize the board, given all the new people who have begun using it. Opposing moderators like zjz, they said, wanted to keep the more homespun feeling of what WallStreetBets used to be.
 “It’s been his life’s work and he’s afraid it will change essentially,” the moderators said.
  “This was our clubhouse with all our friends where we let loose and were honest about being in it for the money, but not being in it to sell each other anything,” zjz wrote by email.
 On Thursday afternoon, Reddit stepped in to remove the top WallStreetBets moderators. They put the moderators who had sided with zjz back in control, though zjz himself was not restored.
 Mr. Cormier, who has been unemployed since March when he lost his job in a shop specializing in the game Magic the Gathering, said he was dismayed by the fighting on WallStreetBets.
 He feared the forum would splinter, making it all but impossible to launch the kind of coordinated effort that had generated such a spectacular market reaction last week.
 “If they kill WallStreetBets, then it’s going to be really tough to restart this effort,” he said.
title: 在争夺电影版权方面，Reddit删除了主要的“ WallStreetBets”主持人
title_en: Amid Bickering Over Movie Rights, Reddit Removes Top 'WallStreetBets' Moderators

note:
乔迪·威廉姆斯（Jody Williams）知道上个月初事情变得一发不可收拾，当时亲特朗普的留言板上塞纳德（TheDonald）上的帖子包括了如何在绞索上打上“ tie子手结”的详细图表。
       威廉姆斯（Williams）是董事会的主持人，也是其网址的所有者，因此他删除了绞索指令。他说，但是在一个小时内，另一位主持人在站点顶部附近悄悄地对其进行了恢复。三天后的1月6日，在美国国会大厦遭到暴力围困的情况下，在国家购物中心的临时绞刑架上悬挂了一个绞索。
    关于绞索图的争斗只是一个被种族主义，反犹太人和暴力内容所困扰的站点中的众多争斗之一，以至于现年41岁的威廉姆斯是一名陆军老兵，在一次非战斗事故中失去了一条腿，常常因特朗普的支持者而退缩说了做。
 威廉姆斯回想起他与一些特别顽固的主持人的交流时说：“成为某些民族主义者可能会很高兴，但我不是。” “我不希望与此有任何关系。”
 TheDonald的故事，是一个疯狂的亲特朗普论坛，已成为国会大厦袭击的在线集结地，这是有关Internet阴暗面的警惕故事。 Reddit最初是针对新贵总统候选人的在线政治集会而开始的，随着威廉姆斯与他所说的“邪恶势力”决意推进包括白人至上主义在内的最极端意识形态的斗争-并且经常失败-变得越来越犯规。
 威廉姆斯（Williams）是去年Reddit将论坛驱逐后控制住论坛地址的网址，在国会大厦袭击事件发生两周后，终于在1月21日采取决定性的行动，此前他得知一些其他主持人已经开始建立自己的网站，用它来攻击他。很快，威廉姆斯（Williams）发挥了自己的权力，成为网址所有者，使TheDonald下线。
 然后，他公开为自己的前同胞辩护，这些同胞曾批评他是“流氓”和自私的co夫。他说，住在德克萨斯州并育有三个孩子的威廉姆斯还遭受了死亡威胁，在线骚扰和联邦调查局的质疑。 
QAnon和亲特朗普在线论坛在Patriots.win上美国国会大厦围困主持人的努力中挣扎和破裂，TheDonald社区的一些成员在天黑后搬到那里，本周未回应置评请求。在新的衍生网站上，他们将威廉姆斯称为“卖点”，“使社区……成百上千的忠实爱国者”。
 分裂了唐纳德（Donald）的分裂提供了强有力的象征，标志着网络上有关言论自由和极端主义的斗争日趋紧张。自frame为言论自由避难所的在线社区经常发现自己陷入边缘，迫使成员要么面对转变，要么容忍日益激进的想法。
 这样的动态在Facebook和其他主流社交媒体网站上已经发挥了作用，但在利基论坛（如Gab，8kun，Parler和TheDonald）上却得到了加强，它们促进了自己成为自由语音区的发展，但最终在网络最糟糕的情况下被贩运。 。
 威廉姆斯（Donald Williams）说，唐纳德（Donald）在12月一天的访问量超过一百万，它可能是最受欢迎的亲特朗普独立站点，它为他的支持者提供了一个不受阻碍的论坛，追随者们可以嘲笑保守派模因和巨魔自由主义者而不必担心关于主流社交媒体网站（如Facebook和Twitter）的内容审核规则。
 但据知情人士透露，在1月6日临近时，有关潜在暴力行为的在线讨论震惊了联邦调查局官员，尤其是关于唐纳德。这些人说，在骚乱发生前一天发布的一份FBI内部报告中引用了该网站上发布的一些暴力和煽动性评论，尽管这些报道并未得到《华盛顿邮报》的评论，但这些名字都提到了唐纳德。
在星期三针对2020年大选的集会之前，亲特朗普论坛爆发了暴力威胁。FBI备忘录中引用的评论之一宣称，特朗普的支持者应前往华盛顿并“采取暴力行动”。停止称此为游行，集会或抗议。去那里准备战争。我们要当总统，否则我们就死。”
 有些人在远处看着塞纳德（TheDonald），他们认为，如果该网站变得如此明显地令人反感，威廉姆斯应该早点采取行动-在FBI打电话之前，在该网站的域名注册商要求清理之前，在塞纳德（Donald）起到鼓动攻击的作用之前造成了四名暴乱者和一名警察死亡，美国民主运动的所在地被洗劫一空。 
“威廉姆斯是否宽恕种族主义和反犹太主义是无关紧要的。他的网站有助于煽动和促进对美国民主的最残酷的攻击之一，”负责监视网络极端主义的SITE Intelligence Group执行总监Rita Katz说。 “他不会假装自己不是问题的一部分。”
 针对这种批评，威廉姆斯表示，他希望特朗普获胜并留在白宫。他不想破坏他花了这么长时间生长的东西。直到最后，他都没有想到是否要炸毁一切。他认为自己作为主持人的行为是一个团队的一部分，该团队平均每天删除3500个有问题的帖子和评论，这有助于阻止最糟糕的事情-同时也帮助了特朗普。
 “我是否为此做出了一些妥协？毫无疑问，”威廉姆斯承认。 “有时候您吞下药丸不喜欢把事情做好。那就是我们生活的世界。。。那是政治。”
众议院监督委员会主席要求联邦调查局对Parler进行调查，包括其在围困国会大厦中的作用。然而，观察家对威廉姆斯如何处理局势感到失望，他很难从互联网的第一线进行举报。
 威廉姆斯近距离观察到QAnon极端主义意识形态，大屠杀否认和非种族主义种族主义。他目睹了令人憎恶的人们散布对儿童的性剥削图像，并淹没了附属图像板，以至于必须将其关闭。
 然后，对于威廉姆斯来说，也许最令人不安的是，他看着极其恐怖的网络演员玩着很长的美容游戏，试图招募不满意的演员。
 威廉姆斯说：“他们就像任何遥远的恐怖组织一样。” “他们认为自己最终会赢得胜利，他们只需要在其中慢慢楔入。” 
他在被删除的推文中说，民主党“共产主义者”在尖叫“为战争而战”，他为“唐纳德”上的一则帖子辩护，其中展示了一盒子弹，以回应“操纵的投票箱”。 1月6日之前的几天，威廉姆斯在推特上说，前往华盛顿特区的特朗普支持者应“围攻试图束缚你们所有人的腐败联邦仪器”，包括标签“ #DCMustFall”。
 威廉姆斯在接受《华盛顿邮报》的提问后删除了自己的Twitter帐户和其他社交媒体，他表示，他在敦促采取政治行动，而不是暴力行为，并坚持挑战他所认为的根深蒂固的，地理上集中的统治阶级。
 他还面临批评，认为他最近的面孔与主持人之间的鲜血或自私的业务计算有关。在“无审查”留言板Ruqqus上，批评家称威廉姆斯是一位渴望权力的“网络管理员”，并补充说：“您完全乐于接受，直到他们开始从您的个人王国转移。 …您试图用鱼雷击打此动作。你太无耻了。”
从社交媒体主流流放，特朗普及其追随者将在网络的极端角落发现生活的不同，但威廉姆斯现在承认，他认为该运动的要素太过分了。在上个月的推文中，他呼吁特朗普放弃“疯狂的”“权利的边缘”，包括QAnon追随者和种族主义者。他写道：“他们为我们付出的代价超过了他们带来的回报。更糟糕的是，它们具有传染性。”
 威廉姆斯表示自己是基督徒和定期教堂的信徒，在总统候选人参议员特德·克鲁兹（R-Tex。）的第一选择后，于2016年8月加入Reddit团体r / TheDonald，在共和党初选中遭到特朗普的否决。 2017年4月，威廉姆斯成为志愿者主持人。
 他手上有时间，还掌握了一些相关技能。在成为现役军人之前，他曾在信息技术领域工作。他因在职事故中的腿部受伤而需要进行约20例手术，这使他成为所谓的“专业患者”。威廉姆斯花了大量时间阅读新闻，并与其他人在线分享他的想法。
 他说，作为Reddit社区的主持人，威廉姆斯因仇恨言论和暴力言论而臭名昭著，他对某些偶然的种族主义以及Reddit违反政策的执法行动不以为然。当该公司实施一系列处罚时（最终导致该论坛在6月被彻底禁止），他帮助将出逃者放到了网络限制较少的区域，购买了包括TheDonald.win在内的数十个潜在地址。 
这个版本的《唐纳德》虽然形式上与Reddit上的版本非常相似，但是却进一步趋向于更加古怪而危险的演讲。
国会大厦的围困计划在线进行。特朗普支持者现在正在计划下一个。去年，一组研究人员在早期的Reddit论坛和独立站点中分析了有关TheDonald的数百万条帖子和评论。他们扫描了所谓的“社会上不受欢迎的内容”，包括性别歧视，种族主义和同性恋仇恨言论，以及讨论有针对性的骚扰和阴谋理论的帖子。
 他们发现，并不是每个人都离开Reddit进入衍生网站-只有最顽固的用户才这么做。他们也往往是最响亮的声音，也最有可能宣扬最狂热的想法。他们建立了一个思想上的回声室，提倡彼此的想法，并将新的或更温和的用户推向极端。
 “在迁移之后，用户变得更加有毒且更加激进，”德国马克斯·普朗克信息学研究所的一位研究人员Savvas Zannettou说。 “这些用户更加活跃，分享了更多有毒的帖子，并且随着时间的推移只会变得更加有毒。”
 社区从Reddit的撤离帮助我们以反对世界的态度吸引了一些成员，许多投票最多的帖子推翻了关于在网上陷入困境的特朗普支持者应如何反击以捍卫总统和自己的想法的界限。 。
 研究小组“反极端主义项目”的约书亚·费舍尔·伯奇（Joshua Fisher-Birch）表示，唐纳德的激进主义说明了在线社区领导人和追随者之间的推动和拉动，这可以使他们成为“极端主义内容和网络的温床”。
 网站主持人根据其保留或删除的内容设置可接受的语调。追随者根据他们的赞扬，评论或庆祝活动来激励某些信息，模因和行为。他说，一个迎合匿名巨魔和游击队员欢迎的留言板注定要突破允许的界限，加剧仇恨言论，性别歧视和白人至上。 
Fisher-Birch说：“这并不是像某人醒来的一天，突然之间，他们的网站已成为仇恨言论和极端主义的集结地。”但是“一个网站不会删除鼓励暴力的内容，实际上只是在鼓励那些张贴者张贴更多相同的内容。”
         威廉姆斯有不同的看法：他发现，在冠状病毒大流行之前，论坛在Reddit和Web上的对话水平都是可以容忍的。他说，由于人们被困在家里，并对公共卫生的限制感到愤怒，因此，关于唐纳德的基调进一步粗化了。随着抗议活动蔓延至抗议警察杀害乔治·弗洛伊德（George Floyd）的事件，又发生了又一次愤怒的转折，用户在不受拘束的保守主义栏杆的打击下反对黑生活（Black Lives Matter），antifa以及论坛评论者认为这是对混乱和破坏的主流容忍。
 特朗普在11月的大选之后，毫无根据地宣称存在广泛的选举欺诈行为，这进一步加剧了对唐纳德的恶毒。
 威廉姆斯说，他越来越意识到自己认为邪恶的演员为扩大网站边界所做的故意努力。他特别震惊地回忆起6月份的一集，其中用户在多个主题上发布了一系列几乎相同的帖子，每个帖子都比上一个明显种族主义。后来在9chan上发布的帖子-甚至更极端的留言板-似乎来自同一位用户，描述了他如何在TheDonald上进行审阅，试图弄清楚他能走多远而不删除其评论。
 9chan上的帖子说：“我相信'唐纳德'可以重传”（这个词的意思是逐渐被说服接受最初被认为是令人反感的想法），使其相信种族主义阴谋论，即“白人灭绝种族”。它毫无根据地声称，有一个国际图谋鼓励少数族裔增加人口，目的是取代白人。
 威廉姆斯说：“他试图让人们采取行动。”他称此评论“激进”和“危险”，并与《邮报》分享了该截图。 “他说的是，我们需要把人们推到一个拐点，在那里他们拿起枪支，找到一个少数民族，然后射击。这就是这些人想要的。”
“没有什么可以阻止即将到来的事情”：激怒了国会大厦骚乱的极右派论坛在威廉姆斯表示欢呼之后说，他在TheDonald以及两个加密消息网站Signal和Telegram的附属社区中的私人消息中看到了其他深深困扰的事情。他认为，在这三个平台上的私人频道都是针对Capitol攻城活动最激进的计划和煽动活动。 
人们聚在一起。他们在这些反向渠道上建立了联系，并且畅所欲言地谈论着他们真正想谈论的话题。”威廉姆斯说。
 威廉姆斯说，在管理《唐纳德》的公众一角时，主持人在如何积极执行站点政策上存在分歧。威廉姆斯说，随后发生了战斗，其中许多失败了。威廉姆斯说，他知道自己有权以Web域所有者的身份彻底杀死该网站，但他也感到自己是一个小组计划的一部分，任何人都不能单方面结束。
 他说，但即使是特朗普的忠实拥护者，特朗普的支持者的场面（几乎可以肯定地在唐纳德见面并组织了他们的人）仍然压倒了国会大厦郁闷的威廉姆斯。
 该网站很快出现在重要新闻报道，刑事调查和对特朗普的弹articles文章中。域名注册商Epik警告说，在有关仇恨和威胁性内容的大量投诉之后，该网站将被下线。
 联邦调查局的来访询问，埃皮克（Epik）和撰写有关唐纳德（Donald）在国会大厦袭击中的角色的记者的报道淹没了威廉姆斯（Williams），为此，对网站进行审核已经成为一项全职工作。威廉姆斯还知道，TheDonald社区的成员确实利用该网站煽动了这次袭击。
 他说：“人们肯定使用该网站进行沟通和协调，”他回应了独立研究人员的结论。
 当被问及现在是否感到遗憾或内时，威廉姆斯接受了前者，但拒绝了后者。而且，他仍然为特朗普对小政府的倡导辩护，同时对他在追随者中得到的一些启发感到不安。 
威廉姆斯说：“大概有40％到50％的特朗普支持者真的相信他是通过QAnon发送眨眼，轻推，轻推消息。” “很多典型的，有理智的美国人都为此而屈服。”
 他现在花时间照顾家人，并试图建立并运行一个新网站America.win。与TheDonald不​​同，它不会提供不受限制的讨论。他说，它将更多地是威廉姆斯认为关于自由市场，个人自由和其他“共同的爱国主义”内容的重要内容的集合。
 他对那些可能仍陷在他曾经加入，后来被审核，然后被杀死的那种论坛中的人说了一个离别信息：事情往往看起来并不像现在。 QAnon不是真实的。在网路上看起来像是神奇，神秘的秘密智慧之声，可能只是一个隐藏在互联网面纱背后的家伙，试图保持一切运转，希望它不会失控。

note_en:
Jody Williams knew things had gotten out of hand early last month, when a post on the pro-Trump message board TheDonald included a detailed diagram of how to tie a “hangman’s knot” on a noose.
       Williams was a moderator for the board and owner of its Web address, so he removed the noose instructions. But within an hour, he said, another moderator quietly restored it near the top of the site. Three days later, on Jan. 6, a real noose was hung on makeshift gallows on the National Mall, amid a violent siege on the U.S. Capitol.
    The battle over the noose diagram was just one of many over a site so infested with racist, anti-Semitic and violent content that Williams, 41, an Army veteran who lost a leg in a noncombat accident, often recoiled at what his fellow Trump supporters said and did.
 “You might be happy being some ethno-nationalist, but I’m not,” said Williams, recalling his exchanges with a handful of particularly hardcore moderators. “I don’t want anything to do with this.”
 The story of TheDonald, a furiously pro-Trump forum that became an online staging ground for the Capitol assault, is a cautionary tale about the Internet’s dark side. What began on Reddit as an online political rally for an upstart presidential candidate turned increasingly foul as Williams fought — and often lost — against what he said were “nefarious forces” determined to advance the most extreme ideologies, including white supremacy.
 Williams — who controlled the Web address where the forum moved after Reddit expelled it last year — finally took decisive action on Jan. 21, two weeks after the Capitol assault, after waking to news that a group of other moderators had started their own site and used it to attack him. Soon, Williams used his power as the Web address owner to knock TheDonald offline.
 Then he defended himself publicly against his former compatriots, who had criticized him as a “rogue” and a selfish coward. Williams, who lives in Texas and has three young children, also endured death threats, online harassment and FBI questioning, he said.
  QAnon and pro-Trump online forums are struggling and fracturing in aftermath of the U.S. Capitol siege Moderators at Patriots.win, where some of TheDonald community moved after it went dark, did not respond to requests for comment this week. On their new spinoff site, they have labeled Williams a “sellout” who “betrayed the community … [of] hundreds of thousands of loyal patriots.”
 The schism that fractured TheDonald offers a potent symbol of the increasingly tense battles over free speech and extremism on the Web. Online communities that frame themselves as refuges for free expression often find themselves pulled to the fringes, forcing members to either confront the shift or tolerate increasingly radical ideas.
 Such dynamics have played out on Facebook and other mainstream social media sites, but they have been supercharged on niche forums such as Gab, 8kun, Parler and TheDonald, which promoted themselves as free-speech zones but ended up trafficking in the worst of the Web.
 TheDonald, which Williams said generated more than a million visits a day in December, was perhaps the most popular pro-Trump stand-alone site, and it offered his supporters a gleefully uninhibited forum where followers could laugh about conservative memes and troll liberals without worrying about the content-moderation rules of mainstream social media sites such as Facebook and Twitter.
 But as Jan. 6 approached, online discussions of potential violence alarmed  FBI officials, especially on TheDonald, according to people familiar with the investigation who spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss the matter. Some of the violent and incendiary comments posted on the site were quoted in an internal FBI report issued a day before the riot, though the report, which was reviewed by The Washington Post, does not mention TheDonald by name, these people said.
Pro-Trump forums erupt with violent threats ahead of Wednesday’s rally against the 2020 election One of the comments cited in the FBI memo declared Trump supporters should go to Washington and get “violent. Stop calling this a march, or rally, or a protest. Go there ready for war. We get our President or we die.”
 Some of those watching TheDonald from afar argue that if the site had grown so obviously repugnant, Williams should have acted sooner — before the FBI came calling, before the site’s domain registrar demanded it be cleaned up, before TheDonald played a role in instigating an attack that left four rioters and a police officer dead and the seat of American democracy ransacked.
 “Whether or not Williams condones racism and anti-Semitism is irrelevant. His site helped incite and facilitate one of the most egregious assaults on American democracy,” said Rita Katz, executive director of SITE Intelligence Group, which monitors online extremism. “He doesn’t get to pretend he wasn’t part of the problem.”
 In response to such critiques, Williams said he wanted Trump to win and stay in the White House. He didn’t want to destroy something he had spent so long growing. He didn’t think, until the very end, that it was necessarily his call whether to blow it all up. He thought his actions as a moderator, part of a team that removed 3,500 problematic posts and comments on an average day, helped keep the worst stuff at bay — while also helping Trump.
 “Did I compromise some of my principles to do so? Without a doubt,” Williams acknowledged. “Sometimes you swallow pills you don’t like to get things done. That’s the world we live in. … That’s politics.”
House Oversight Committee chairwoman requests FBI probe of Parler, including its role in Capitol siege However observers come down on how Williams handled the situation, he has troubling things to report from the front lines of the Internet.
 Williams saw, up close, the QAnon extremist ideology, Holocaust denial and unalloyed racism. He witnessed repellent people spreading images of child sexual exploitation, flooding an affiliated image board so thoroughly that it had to be shut down.
 And then, perhaps most unnervingly for Williams, he watched terrifyingly extreme online actors playing a very long game of grooming, seeking to recruit the disaffected to their causes.
 “They’re like any far-flung terrorist group,” Williams said. “They think they’re going to win eventually, and they just need to slowly drive a wedge in there.”
   In since-deleted tweets, he said that Democratic “communists” were screaming “for war,” and he defended a post on TheDonald showing a box of bullets as a response to the “rigged ballot box.” Days before Jan. 6, Williams tweeted that Trump supporters going to D.C. should “siege the corrupt Federal Apparatus that seeks to chain you all up,” including the hashtag “#DCMustFall.”
 Williams, who deleted his Twitter account and other social media after questions about them from The Washington Post, said he was urging political action, not violence, and persistence in challenging what he saw as an entrenched, geographically concentrated ruling class.
 He also has faced criticism that his recent about-face had more to do with bad blood between moderators or a self-interested business calculation. On the “no censorship” message board Ruqqus, critics called Williams an “Internet janitor” with a lust for power, adding, “You were totally happy to be hands off until they started redirecting from your personal kingdom. … You tried to torpedo this movement. Shame on you.”
Exiled from social media mainstream, Trump and his followers will find life different at the extreme corners of the Web But Williams now recognizes, he says, that elements of the movement went too far. In tweets last month, he called on Trump to disown the “insane” “fringes of the ‘right,’ ” including QAnon followers and racists. He wrote, “They are costing us more than they could ever bring to the table. Worse, they are contagious.”
 Williams, who said he’s a Christian and a regular churchgoer, joined the Reddit group r/TheDonald in August 2016, after his first choice in presidential candidates, Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Tex.), got trounced by Trump in the Republican primaries. In April 2017, Williams became a volunteer moderator.
 He had time on his hands and some relevant skills. Before becoming an active-duty member of the Army, he had worked in information technology. His leg injury from an accident while on active duty required about 20 surgeries, making him what he called “a professional patient.” Williams spent a lot of time reading news and sharing his thoughts with others online.
 As a moderator for a Reddit community notorious for its hate speech and loose talk of violence, Williams bristled at some of the casual racism but also at Reddit’s enforcement actions for policy violations, he said. When the company imposed a series of penalties — eventually leading to the forum’s outright  banning in June — he helped plot the exodus to less-restrictive areas of the Web, buying up dozens of potential addresses, including  TheDonald.win.
 This version of TheDonald, while very similar in form to what had lived on Reddit, spiraled further toward more outlandish and dangerous speech.
The Capitol siege was planned online. Trump supporters now planning the next one. A team of researchers last year  analyzed millions of posts and comments on TheDonald, both in its earlier Reddit forum and its stand-alone site. They scanned for what they called “socially undesirable content,” including sexist, racist and homophobic slurs and posts discussing targeted harassment and conspiracy theories.
 What they found is not everyone left Reddit for the spinoff site — only the most hardcore users did. They also tended to be the loudest voices and the most likely to preach the most fanatical ideas. They fostered an ideological echo chamber, boosting one another’s ideas and pushing new or more moderate users further into the extreme.
 “After the migration, users became more toxic and more radicalized,” said one of the researchers, Savvas Zannettou, of the  Max Planck Institute for Informatics in Germany. “These users were more active, were sharing more toxic posts, and only got more toxic over time.”
 The community’s removal from Reddit helped draw some members together with an us-against-the-world attitude, and many of the top-voted posts pushed the boundaries with ideas on how beleaguered Trump supporters online should fight back in defense of the president and themselves.
 Joshua Fisher-Birch of the  Counter Extremism Project, a research group, said TheDonald’s radicalization illustrated the push and pull between online communities’ leaders and followers that can turn them into “hotbeds for extremist content and networking.”
 Site moderators set the tone for what is acceptable based on what they keep or remove. And followers incentivize certain messages, memes and behaviors based on what they upvote, comment on or celebrate. An anything-goes message board catering to an audience of anonymous trolls and partisans is destined to push the boundaries of what is permissible, fueling hate speech, sexism and white supremacy, he said.
 “It’s not like someone wakes up one day and, all of a sudden, their site has become this cesspool of hate speech and extremism,” Fisher-Birch said. But “a site that doesn’t remove content that encourages violence is really just encouraging more of the same content by those posters.”
         Williams has a different perspective: He found the forum’s level of conversation — on both Reddit and the Web incarnation — tolerable until the coronavirus pandemic hit. With people stuck at home and angered by public-health restrictions, the tone on TheDonald further coarsened, he said. It took another, still-angrier turn as protests spread against the police killing of George Floyd, with users on this bastion of unfettered conservatism railing against Black Lives Matter, antifa and what forum commenters viewed as mainstream tolerance for mayhem and destruction.
 The November election, followed by Trump’s baseless claims of widespread electoral fraud, further intensified the viciousness on TheDonald.
 Williams said he’d become increasingly aware of what he believed were intentional efforts by nefarious actors to push the site’s boundaries. He recalled with particular horror an episode from June in which a user posted a series of nearly identical posts on several threads, each more explicitly racist than the last. A later post on 9chan — an even more extreme message board — appeared to be from the same user describing how he was probing the moderation on TheDonald, trying to figure out exactly how far he could go without getting his comments deleted.
 The post on 9chan said, “I believe ‘TheDonald’ can be redpilled” — a term meaning gradually convinced to embrace ideas that initially would be considered repugnant — into believing a racist conspiracy theory called “white genocide.” It baselessly claims there is an international plot encouraging population growth by minority groups, with the goal of replacing White people.
 “He was trying to get people to act,” said Williams, who called the comment “radical” and “dangerous” and shared a screenshot of it with The Post. “He’s saying we need to push people to an inflection point, where they pick up their gun, find a minority person and shoot. That’s what these people want.”
‘Nothing can stop what’s coming’: Far-right forums that fomented Capitol riots voice glee in aftermath Williams said he saw other deeply troubling things in private messages on TheDonald and on affiliated communities on Signal and Telegram, two encrypted messaging sites. He believes private channels on all three platforms hosted the most aggressive planning and instigation for the Capitol siege.
 “People got together. They linked up on these back channels, and they talked about what they really wanted to talk about, without prying eyes,” Williams said.
 In managing the public-facing part of TheDonald, Williams said moderators were deeply split on how aggressively to enforce site policies. Battles ensued, many of which he lost, Williams said. He knew he had the power to kill the site outright as the owner of the Web domain but also felt he was part of a group project that no one person should unilaterally end, Williams said.
 But even as a Trump loyalist, scenes of Trump’s supporters — some of whom almost certainly met and organized themselves on TheDonald — overrunning the Capitol depressed Williams, he said.
 The site soon featured in critical news reports, criminal investigations and articles of impeachment for Trump. The domain registrar, Epik, warned that the site would get kicked offline after a flood of complaints about hateful, threatening content.
 Incoming queries from the FBI, Epik and journalists writing about TheDonald’s role in the Capitol attack inundated Williams, for whom moderating the site already had become something of a full-time job. Williams also knew that members of TheDonald community had indeed used the site to instigate the assault.
 “People definitely used the site to communicate and coordinate,” he said, echoing the conclusions of independent researchers.
 Asked whether he now feels regret or guilt, Williams accepts the former but rejects the latter. And he still defends Trump’s advocacy for smaller government, while being uneasy with some of what he inspired in followers.
 “Probably 40 to 50 percent of Trump supporters really believe he’s sending wink-wink, nudge-nudge messages through QAnon,” Williams said. “A lot of typical, reasonable Americans have fallen for it.”
 He now is spending his time caring for family and trying to get a new site, America.win, up and running. Unlike TheDonald, it will not offer unfettered discussion. It will be, he said, more of an aggregator of what Williams considers important content about free markets, individual liberty and other “common patriotic causes.”
 He has a parting message for those who might still be caught up in the roiling forums of the sort he once joined, then moderated, then killed off: Things often are not as they seem. QAnon is not real. What may look online like a magical, mystical voice of secret wisdom may just be a guy hiding behind the Internet’s veil, trying to keep it all going, hoping it doesn’t spin out of control.

note:
消息传出后，比特币的价格立即上涨了约7％，达到每枚代币40,000美元以上。
 特斯拉此前曾发电表示，它对加密货币感兴趣，但是购买如此大的硬币很值得注意。
   特斯拉在文件中写道，今年早些时候，它“更新了[其]投资政策，为[其]提供了更大的灵活性，以进一步分散并最大化其[其]不需要维持足够的运营流动性的现金收益”，并补充说它可以选择将现金放入“某些另类储备资产”中，其中包括“数字资产，金条，黄金交易所交易基金和未来指定的其他资产”。
 在这个旗帜下，该公司“已在比特币上总共投资了15亿美元”，并继续说著名的电动汽车公司“可能会不时或长期收购和持有数字资产。”
 特斯拉有足够的回旋余地，可以利用其现金和加密货币市场做任何想做的事情。
 但是该公司并未完成，而是通过补充说该公司“期望在不久的将来开始接受比特币作为其产品的付款方式，但要遵守适用的法律，并在最初以有限的基础，[在收到后可能会或可能不会清算。”
 特斯拉（Tesla）首席执行官埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）在最近几天掀起了一场愚蠢的笑话，即狗狗币（Dogecoin），引起了轰动。这更重要。特斯拉选择比特币作为其选择的加密货币，从而进一步巩固了区块链作为全球最知名的地位。尽管我们可能会重复一遍，但将来它可以接受以比特币计价的交易可以帮助比特币保留价值和交易量。值得一提的是，马斯克本人过去也曾利用自己的社交形象亲自提高了比特币的价格，包括将自己的生物更改为一个单词，然后在本月初将其删除后价格回落。 
然后，汽车公司用了三段文字说它的选择是有风险的。 轻描淡写。 再说一遍，如果不去娱乐，麝香是什么？
note_en:
As the news broke, the price of bitcoin instantly rose by around 7% to more than $40,000 per coin.
 Tesla had  previously telegraphed that it had an interest in the cryptocurrency, however to purchase such a large block of the coin is notable.
   In its filing, Tesla writes that earlier this year it “updated [its] investment policy to provide [it] with more flexibility to further diversify and maximize returns on [its] cash that is not required to maintain adequate operating liquidity,” adding that it has the option of putting cash into “certain alternative reserve assets” that include “digital assets, gold bullion, gold exchange-traded funds and other assets as specified in the future.”
 Under that banner, the firm has “invested an aggregate $1.50 billion in bitcoin,” going on to say that the well-known electric car company “may acquire and hold digital assets from time to time or long-term.”
 That’s enough wiggle room for Tesla to do whatever it wants with its cash and the crypto markets.
 But the company wasn’t done, completing its news-drop by adding that the company “expect[s] to begin accepting bitcoin as a form of payment for [its] products in the near future, subject to applicable laws and initially on a limited basis, which [it] may or may not liquidate upon receipt.”
 Tesla CEO Elon Musk has made waves in recent days by  pumping a silly cryptocurrency joke called Dogecoin; this is something more material. Tesla is selecting bitcoin as the cryptocurrency of its choice, helping to further cement the blockchain as the world’s best known. And that it may accept bitcoin-denominated transactions in the future could help bitcoin retain both value and exchange volume, though we probably repeat ourselves. It’s worth noting that Musk himself has also personally sent bitcoin prices higher in the past using his social presence, including by changing his bio to just the single word,  before its price faded back after he removed it earlier this month.
 The car company then spends three paragraphs saying that its choice is risky. That’s an understatement. Then again, what is Musk if not entertaining?

note_en:
Iran is running two surveillance operations in cyber-space, targeting more than 1,000 dissidents, according to a leading cyber-security company.
 The efforts were directed against individuals in Iran and 12 other countries, including the UK and US, Check Point said.
 It said the two groups involved were using new techniques to install spyware on targets' PCs and mobile devices.
  One of the groups, known as Domestic Kitten or APT-50, is accused of tricking people into downloading malicious software on to mobile phones by a variety of means including:
 repackaging an existing version of an authentic video game found on the Google Play store
 The American-Israeli company's researchers documented 1,200 victims being targeted by the campaign, living in seven countries.
   The second group, known as Infy or Prince Of Persia, is said to spy on the home and work PCs of dissidents in 12 countries, extracting sensitive data after tricking people into opening malicious email attachments.
    And Check Point said there was evidence it had run at least 10 campaigns since 2017.
   And it was using an Iranian blog site, Telegram channels and text messages to lure people into installing its infected software, which the researchers have dubbed Furball, which could:
  The 600 successful infections are said to have included dissidents, opposition forces and people belonging to the Kurdish ethnic minority in:
  The other group, Infy, is said to have been operating as far back as 2007.
 Its most recent activity had targeted PCs, with fake emails with attractive content, usually with an attached document, Check Point said.
 One example provided was of a document apparently about loans being offered to disabled veterans.
  Once the document was opened, a spying tool was installed and sensitive data stolen, the company said.
  Two documents recently used are said to have included a photo of an Iranian governor, with alleged contact details.
 The researchers said Infy's capabilities were "far superior" to most other known Iranian campaigns, thanks to its ability to be highly selective about its targets and to have generally have gone undetected.
 "It is clear that the Iranian government is investing significant resources into cyber-operations," Check Point cyber-research head Yaniv Balmas said.
 "The operators of these Iranian cyber-espionage campaigns seem to be completely unaffected by any counter-activities done by others, even though both campaigns had been revealed and even stopped in the past.

马克·库班（Mark Cuban）参与了音频和播客的炒作。根据发送给The Verge看到的可能的创作者合作伙伴的电子邮件，他与共同创始人Falon Fatemi一起计划推出Fireside，这是一个“下一代播客平台”，可促进实时对话。该应用程序的想法类似于嗡嗡声的现场音频创业公司Clubhouse，除了能够本地记录对话。一位与Fireside接近的消息人士称，该应用计划于今年公开发布。
 法蒂米（Fatemi）是AI客户关系服务节点的共同创立者和销售者，他承诺提供一个平台，在此平台上，创作者将能够使用Fireside的内置分析工具来广播，记录并从中获利，同时可以确定哪种内容效果最佳。根据现已到期的工程师职位发布，该应用程序已经筹集了“数百万美元”的种子资金，可以开始使用。接近该公司的消息人士说，将为创作者提供各种交易和赚钱方式，而且该应用不会让任何人公开发表演讲。这将是一次精心策划的体验。
  该创业公司似乎仍处于隐身模式。目前尚不清楚其成立时间，但最早的职位发布早在9月份就开始了。 Fatemi于8月离开Node，当时表示她将继续从事“新事业”。她尚未在自己的LinkedIn上列出Fireside。 Cuban和Fatemi之前在Node上合作，由Cuban投资该产品。他在Fireside的共同创始人身份使他重返音频领域。他和托德·瓦格纳（Todd Wagner）在1999年以57亿美元的价格将互联网广播公司Broadcast.com卖给了雅虎，这是著名的。
 “今天，我们告别了单向对话，缺乏互动性和不存在的分析所造成的当前媒体平台限制，” Fatemi在写给创作者的电子邮件中写道。 “我们通过根据创作者的发言权（而不是大喊大叫）来赋予创作者权力，从而达到公平的竞争环境，我们为他们的想法提供了触角，将一次对话变成下一个媒体帝国的种子。”
 Fireside的职位发布声称“社交媒体使我们失败了”，方法是创建“带有用于交流的媒介的对立回声室，这种媒介只会加剧激怒传播信息的愤怒文化和商业模式。”该职位称，Fireside希望通过其广播平台促进“民间智慧的话语，共享的联系体验以及真正的人际关系建立”，这将“通过大规模的有意义的对话来促进社会影响。”
  作为该目标的一部分，法塔米（Fatemi）正在与已建立的Podcast创作者联系，以了解“ Founding Firesider”景点的建立，似乎是在建立Fireside用户的初始名单。
 法塔米（Fatemi）拒绝置评，古巴（Cuban）证实“是”，他是联合创始人。 
该应用程序与已建立的播客托管和分析平台Fireside无关，该平台自2016年以来一直存在。Fireside的创始人兼首席执行官Dan Benjamin表示，这两项服务之间没有任何联系。他写道：“创建一个令人难忘的品牌是一项挑战，我们已经听到对此感到困惑的客户（和潜在客户）的信息。” “这令人惊讶，而且不仅仅涉及。我想他们只是没有意识到我们在Fireside的主持播客已经有五年了，或者自2006年以来我个人参与播客。当然，我邀请他们与我联系，以便我们开始对话并找出解决方案。”
 Cuban和Fatemi的Fireside加入了许多其他音频应用程序，这些应用程序希望使实时音频成为常规的在线交互方式。最著名的俱乐部会所（Clubhouse）于去年成立，并一直稳步增长，即使是仅提供邀请的服务，也吸引了像埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）这样的大人物。该平台目前拥有数百万用户，其团队正在寻求帮助创作者通过正式计划获利。 Twitter还正在测试Spaces，它自己进行直播的对话。尽管这些不是正式的播客应用程序-无法本地录制俱乐部会谈-但它们似乎都利用了Spotify大力提倡的播客炒作。
 Spotify已经投资了数亿美元，以收购成熟的播客网络和技术，并锁定与乔·罗根和奥巴马家族等大牌明星的独家交易。同时，据报道，最初的播客创建者苹果公司正在探索推出播客订阅服务，亚马逊音乐和Audible也正在投资将播客引入其平台。
note_en:
Mark Cuban is getting in on the audio and podcasting hype. Alongside co-founder Falon Fatemi, he’s planning to launch  Fireside, a “next-gen podcast platform” that facilitates live conversation, according to an email sent to possible creator partners seen by  The Verge. The app idea is similar to the buzzy live audio startup Clubhouse, except with the ability to natively record conversations. A source close to Fireside says the app plans to launch publicly this year.
 Fatemi, who co-founded and  sold the AI customer relations service Node, promises a platform where creators will be able to broadcast, record, and monetize conversations while using Fireside’s built-in analytics tools to figure out what content performs best. The app, according to a now-expired engineer job posting, has raised a “multi-million dollar” seed round to get started. The source close to the company says creators will be offered various deals and ways to monetize, and the app won’t let just anyone speak publicly. It’ll be a highly curated experience.
  The startup is still seemingly in stealth mode. It’s unclear when it was founded, but job postings went live as early as September. Fatemi left Node in August, saying at the time that she would be moving on to a “new venture.” She hasn’t listed Fireside on her LinkedIn yet. Cuban and Fatemi previously collaborated on Node, with Cuban investing in the product. His co-founder status on Fireside brings him back into the audio space. He and Todd Wagner  famously sold internet radio company Broadcast.com for $5.7 billion in 1999 to Yahoo.
 “Today we say goodbye to current media platform limitations created by one-way conversations, a lack of interactivity, and non-existent analytics,” Fatemi writes in the email to creators. “We level the playing field by empowering creators based on what they have to say (not how loud they yell) and we give their ideas the reach to turn a single conversation into the seed of the next media empire.”
 Fireside’s job posting claims “social media has failed us” by creating “antagonistic echo chambers with mediums for communication that only further an outrage culture and business models that incentivize spreading disinformation.” Fireside, the posting says, is looking to foster “civil intelligent discourse, shared connected experiences, and genuine relationship building virtually” through its broadcasting platform, which will “promote driving social impact through meaningful conversations at scale.”
  As part of that goal, Fatemi is reaching out to established podcast creators about “Founding Firesider” spots, seemingly to build an initial roster of Fireside users.
 Fatemi declined to comment, and Cuban confirmed that “yes,” he is a co-founder.
 The app is unrelated to the established podcast hosting and analytics platform  Fireside, which has been around since 2016. Dan Benjamin, Fireside’s founder and CEO, says there’s no connection between the two services. “Creating a memorable brand is a challenge, and we’ve already heard from customers (and potential customers) who are confused about this,” he writes to  The Verge. “It’s surprising and more than a bit concerning. I imagine they just didn’t realize that we here at Fireside have been in podcast hosting for almost five years, or about my own personal involvement in podcasting since 2006. Of course I invite them to reach out to me so we can begin a dialogue and figure out a solution.”
 Cuban and Fatemi’s Fireside joins a legion of other audio apps looking to make live audio a regular way to interact online. Clubhouse, most notably, launched last year and has steadily grown, even as an invitation-only service, and  attracted big names like Elon Musk. The platform  now has millions of users, and its team is looking to help creators monetize through a formal program. Twitter is also  testing Spaces, its own take on live broadcasted conversations. While these aren’t formal podcasting apps — there’s no way to record a Clubhouse conversation natively — they all seem to take advantage of the podcasting hype buoyed by Spotify.
 Spotify has invested hundreds of millions of dollars  to acquire established podcasting networks and technology, as well as to lock down exclusive deals with big-name stars, like  Joe Rogan and  the Obama family. Meanwhile, Apple, the original podcast creator, is  reportedly exploring the launch of a podcast subscription service, and  Amazon Music and  Audible are also investing in bringing podcasts to their platforms.

note_en:
CloudLinux is proud to announce the release of AlmaLinux Beta. We’ve collected community feedback and built our new beta release around what you would expect from an enterprise-level Linux distribution. AlmaLinux is a completely free 1:1 binary compatible fork of Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 8, inspired by the community and built by the engineers and talent behind CloudLinux. Visit  this link  to download Beta images.
  With the Beta release deployed, we’d like to ask the community to be involved and provide feedback. We aim to build a Linux distribution entirely from community contributions and feedback. During AlmaLinux Beta, we ask for assistance in testing, documentation, support and future direction for the operating system. Together, we can build a Linux distribution that fills the gap left by the now unsupported CentOS distribution.
 The AlmaLinux team set up all the necessary infrastructure to make it convenient for our contributors to provide their input. The public repository on   Github  is where we will finalize the source code for the system, and any additional documentation will be posted on the   wiki . We set up the wiki and repository to make it easy for the community to provide as much information and feedback as possible. The AlmaLinux team will review every comment and request, but we ask that only registered contributors   file bug reports  to filter out spam.
 To facilitate communication and help answer some of the common questions you might already have, we will be hosting a live QA webinar with the AlmaLinux team.  The webinar will take place on February 10,  5 PM (UTC) / 9 AM (PST). Among the participants present will be Igor Seletskiy, CEO of CloudLinux, and Alexander Vinogradov, Head of Engineering at AlmaLinux.
  If you have any questions, comments, feedback or concerns, please feel free to send us your thoughts to    [email protected]  or send them during the webinar on Twitter with the hashtag #AlmaLinuxBeta.
 CloudLinux and the AlmaLinux team would like to thank the community for their input. This is just the beginning for AlmaLinux, and we look forward to continued improvements and updates for the next generation enterprise-level Linux operating system.
 Thank you for your contribution, input, and support throughout launching AlmaLinux! The infrastructure was the first step, now we need your help to make the next one!
     CloudLinux is proud to present the beta version of AlmaLinux. After roughly a month and a half from the announcement, here is a 1:1 RHEL binary compatible replacement for your RHEL-based systems.
 This is for the community and by the community, you’re the soul of Linux. Thank you for your interest and suggestions so far, keep them coming.
 Use this version to thoroughly test your workloads and report any unintended features (ie, bugs) you may find, it will help make AlmaLinux better.
     AlmaLinux-8.3-beta-1-x86_64-dvd1.iso  – a full installation DVD image that contains mostly all AlmaLinux packages. We don’t really recommend using it unless you need to set up and use AlmaLinux on a machine without internet access.
  Download a preferable ISO image and verify its checksum. Here is an example for GNU/Linux:
 # download and import the AlmaLinux public key $ wget   https://repo.almalinux.org/almalinux/RPM-GPG-KEY-AlmaLinux $ gpg --import RPM-GPG-KEY-AlmaLinux # download a checksums list $ wget   https://repo.almalinux.org/almalinux/8.3-beta/isos/x86_64/CHECKSUM # verify the checksums list, we are looking for “Good signature” $ gpg --verify CHECKSUM  gpg: Signature made Thu 28 Jan 2021 11:39:12 PM MSK gpg:                using RSA key 51D6647EC21AD6EA gpg:  Good signature from "AlmaLinux < [email protected]>" [unknown] gpg: WARNING: This key is not certified with a trusted signature! gpg:          There is no indication that the signature belongs to the owner. Primary key fingerprint: 5E9B 8F56 17B5 066C E920  57C3 488F CF7C 3ABB 34F8      Subkey fingerprint: E53C F5EF 91CE B0AD 1812  ECB8 51D6 647E C21A D6EA # download the network install ISO $ wget   https://repo.almalinux.org/almalinux/8.3-beta/isos/x86_64/AlmaLinux-8.3-beta-1-x86_64-boot.iso # calculate the downloaded ISO SHA256 checksum $ sha256sum AlmaLinux-8.3-beta-1-x86_64-boot.iso  d15be406f417e81382b46a54d87dff01c8ca770c847c18703f19146587b61a1f  AlmaLinux-8.3-beta-1-x86_64-boot.iso # compare it with expected checksum, it should be the same $ cat CHECKSUM | grep -E 'SHA256.*AlmaLinux-8.3-beta-1-x86_64-boot.iso' SHA256 (AlmaLinux-8.3-beta-1-x86_64-boot.iso) = d15be406f417e81382b46a54d87dff01c8ca770c847c18703f19146587b61a1f
  If you decided to use the AlmaLinux-8.3-beta-1-x86_64-boot.iso image, you will need to provide this   https://repo.almalinux.org/almalinux/8.3-beta/BaseOS/x86_64/kickstart/  repository as the Installation Source:
    If you are going to install a non-minimal environment, you will need to add the AppStream repository to the additional repositories:   https://repo.almalinux.org/almalinux/8.3-beta/AppStream/x86_64/os/ .
 There are no extra Installation Sources required if you decided to go with AlmaLinux-8.3-beta-1-x86_64-minimal.iso or AlmaLinux-8.3-beta-1-x86_64-dvd1.iso images.
       Our libreport/abrt packages aren’t integrated with the bugs.almalinux.org bug-tracker yet, so a user will have to submit a crash report manually. Issue:   almbz#2 .
 The “perl:5.30” module support is incomplete in the beta release, it will be finished in the stable.
 We don’t have the latest “jmc” and “maven” module versions. They will be updated later.
 The “satellite-5-client” module is located in the BaseOS repository instead of the AppStream. Issue:   almbz#4 .
  The debuginfo repositories are empty and will be populated in a couple of days after the beta release.

 尽管“太空部队”的网站上没有公开的品牌宣传活动，但雷蒙德将军近几个月来已经与记者打了电话，并参与了媒体露面，以表明他相信新的军事部门如何维护国家安全。
 白宫新闻秘书詹·普萨基（Jen Psaki）周三证实，在拜登（Biden）政府因对新军事分支开个玩笑而遭到共和党议员的抨击后，它将保留太空部队。
 ＆＃34;他们绝对得到拜登政府的全力支持。而且，我们没有重新考虑建立太空部队的决定。普萨基星期三在白宫新闻发布会上说。
 前总统唐纳德·特朗普（Donald Trump）成立太空部队是2019年国防支出法案的一部分，这一行动最初遭到政客和深夜喜剧演员的嘲笑和嘲弄。
 然而，现在这是一个完整的军事部门，在2021年预算中分配了154亿美元，并招募了16,000名现役和文职人员，他们全部从已退役的空军太空司令部调任。陆军和海军人员将从2022财年开始转移到太空部队，该财年将从今年9月开始。 
普萨基似乎是在开玩笑，以回应有关拜登政府是否会在周二下午的新闻发布会上保留太空部队的问题。
 ＆＃34;哇，太空部队。今天是飞机，普萨基开玩笑地说。她接着说，她将调查寻找与太空部队联系的行政人员，并查看新军事部门是否有任何更新。
 几位共和党议员对新闻秘书选择隐瞒军事分支感到愤怒。
 ＆＃34;太空部队是我们武装部队的正式分支机构，拜登政府走出去，在电视上公开嘲笑它，以供所有人观看？他们对服务对象的不尊重程度是可耻的，＆＃34; R-Colo众议员Lauren Boebert在星期二晚些时候的Twitter上说。
 许多专家对乔·拜登总统将太空部队保留为自己的军事部门并不感到惊讶，因为当它得到两党支持作为未来军事努力的宝贵工具时，国会将采取一项行动来废除它。 2020年，由两党代表组成的国会团体组成了众议院和参议院太空力量高加索人联盟。
 ＆＃34;建立太空部队核心小组是朝着寻求共同立场，支持我们的国防以及迈入科学发现与创新的新时代迈出的重要的第一步，参议员太空部队核心小组于2020年9月宣布后的一份声明中说，美国亚利桑那州参议员凯尔斯顿·西内玛（Kyrsten Sinema）在一份声明中表示。
 战略与国际研究中心航空航天安全项目的研究助理马克纳·杨（Makena Young）表示，尽管太空部队将发挥重要作用，但该部门将很难与特朗普及其成立之初的嘲弄脱离联系。 
＆＃34;名字很有趣；听起来像特朗普刚刚梦dream以求的事情，＆＃34;杨说。但这已经在国家安全界谈论了十多年了。向前迈出重要的一步。
  ＆＃34; USSF是一种组织，训练和装备太空部队的军事部门，目的是保护美国和盟国在太空中的利益，并为联合部队提供太空能力。美国海军的职责包括发展《卫报》，获得军事太空系统，使太空力量的军事学说成熟以及组织太空部队向我们的战斗司令部提出。＆＃34;太空部队服务人员被称为监护人。
 布鲁金斯学会安全，战略与技术中心高级研究员兼联合主任弗兰克·罗斯（Frank A. Rose）说，创建太空部队是为了解决一个关键问题。
 ＆＃34;太空部队是认真解决这一严重问题的尝试，而这个问题是俄罗斯和中国等国部署反卫星武器的问题。不幸的是，特朗普政府将其政治化，罗斯说。
 除了保护卫星外，太空部队在维持对美国人至关重要的服务方面也起着至关重要的作用。日常生活。
 太空部队发言人尼克·默库里奥少校说，为智能手机等设备提供导航和定位服务的GPS卫星是由美国太空部队的监护人操作的。
 他接着说，该服务提供的GPS卫星可能比美国人平均认为的更为重要。 
＆＃34;导航功能也许更为知名-可以启用紧急服务，地图应用程序和商业运输，仅举几例-来自GPS的精确定时信号已经融入到日常生活中。 GPS计时支持金融交易，例如在加油站付款，使用ATM或在证券交易所交易证券，＆＃34; 水星说。

WASHINGTON, Feb. 5 (UPI) -- The U.S. Space Force's top general said Wednesday that Americans generally find it "hard to understand" how the Space Force affects them, a day after White House press secretary  Jen Psaki made light of what experts say is a vital military branch.
 "Space doesn't have a mother. You can't reach out and hug a satellite, you can't see it, you can't touch it," Gen. Jay Raymond, the chief of space operations, said to reporters on a press call. "But there is a very, very, very strong connection between those capabilities and our way of life."
 While Space Force does not have a public branding campaign listed on its website, Gen. Raymond has had calls with reporters and engaged in media appearances in recent months to show how he believes the new military branch safeguards national security.
 White House press secretary Jen Psaki confirmed Wednesday that the Biden administration will keep Space Force after she came under fire from Republican lawmakers for appearing to make a joke about the new military branch.
 "They absolutely have the full support of the Biden administration. And we are not revisiting the decision to establish the Space Force," Psaki said Wednesday at a White House news briefing.
 Former President  Donald Trump established Space Force as part of the 2019 defense spending bill, an action that was initially met with ridicule and mockery by politicians and late-night comedians alike.
 However, it is now a full military branch that was allocated $15.4 billion in the 2021 budget and enlisted 16,000 active duty and civilian personnel who were all reassigned from the defunct Air Force Space Command. Army and Navy personnel will begin to be transferred to Space Force in the 2022 fiscal year, which begins this coming September.
 Psaki appeared to make a joke in response to a question about whether the Biden administration will keep Space Force during a news conference Tuesday afternoon.
 "Wow, Space Force. It's the plane of today," Psaki said in a joking tone. She went on to say she would look into finding who the Space Force contact is for the administration and seeing if there were any updates in regard to the new military branch.
 Several Republican lawmakers were outraged that the press secretary chose to make light of a branch of the military.
 "The Space Force is an official branch of our armed services and the Biden administration goes out and openly mocks it on television for all to see? The level of disrespect they have for those who serve is disgraceful," said Rep. Lauren Boebert, R-Colo., on Twitter later Tuesday.
 Many experts were not surprised that President  Joe Biden will keep Space Force as its own branch of the military because it would take an act of Congress to abolish it when it now has bipartisan support as a valuable tool in future military efforts. In 2020, groups of bipartisan members of Congress formed House and Senate Space Force Caucuses.
 "Establishing the Space Force Caucus is an important first step toward finding common ground, supporting our national defense, and embarking on a new era of scientific discovery and innovation," said Sen. Kyrsten Sinema, D-Ariz., in a statement after the Senate Space Force Caucus was announced in September 2020.
 Makena Young, a research associate with the Aerospace Security Project at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, said that while Space Force will serve an important function, the branch will have a difficult time dissociating itself with Trump and the mockery around its inception.
 "The name is funny; it sounds like something that Trump just dreamed up," said Young. "But it's been talked about in national security circles for over a decade now. It's something that's just going to be important to have going forward."
  "The USSF is a military service that organizes, trains, and equips space forces in order to protect U.S. and allied interests in space and to provide space capabilities to the joint force. USSF responsibilities include developing Guardians, acquiring military space systems, maturing the military doctrine for space power, and organizing space forces to present to our Combatant Commands." Space Force service members are known as guardians.
 Frank A. Rose, senior fellow and co-director of the Center for Security, Strategy and Technology at the  Brookings Institution, said Space Force was created to address a critical issue.
 "The Space Force is a serious attempt to deal with a serious problem, and that problem is the deployment of anti-satellite weapons by countries like Russia and China. And it's unfortunate that the Trump administration politicized it," Rose said.
 Other than safeguarding satellites, Space Force also plays a vital role in maintaining services pivotal to Americans' everyday lives.
 Space Force spokesman Maj. Nick Mercurio said that GPS satellites that give navigation and positioning services to devices such as smartphones are operated by guardians of the U.S. Space Force.
 He went on to say that the service's GPS satellites provide may be more important than the average American thinks.
 "While the navigation function is perhaps more well-known -- enabling emergency services, map applications, and commercial shipping, to name a few -- the precise timing signal from GPS is woven into the fabric of daily life. GPS timing underpins financial transactions such as paying at the gas pump, using an ATM, or trading securities in a stock exchange," Mercurio said.
title: 尽管提出了有趣的名字想法，但美国太空部队的任务却很认真
title_en: Despite Funny Name Ideas, US Space Force Has a Serious Mission
